1: 'Hear this word, you cows of Bashan on Mount Samaria.' The 'cows of Bashan' was a striking insult: Bashan (modern Golan Heights) was famous for its fat, well-fed cattle (Psalms 22:12; Ezekiel 39:18; Deuteronomy 32:14). Amos applies this image to the wealthy women of Samaria who 'oppress the poor and crush the needy and say to their husbands, Bring us wine to drink.' The social analysis is specific: the wealthy women's demand for wine drives their husbands to economic exploitation. This connection of elite female consumption to systemic poverty reflects the archaeological evidence of Samarian wealth stratification in the 8th century BCE.
2: 'The Lord GOD has sworn by his holiness' — the divine oath by YHWH's own holiness is the highest possible guarantor; YHWH cannot swear by anything greater than himself (cf. Hebrews 6:13). The image of being dragged away 'with fishhooks' (betzinot, possibly fishhooks or thorns) and 'on fishhooks' (besirot dugah) is disputed in translation but clearly describes captive deportation — perhaps with nose-rings or similar instruments used in ANE deportation records (Assyrian reliefs show captives led by ropes attached to nose/lip rings). The Assyrian deportation of the northern kingdom's population in 722-720 BCE would fulfill this threat.
3: 'You will be dragged through the breaches in the walls, each woman straight out before her, and flung into Harmon.' 'Harmon' (haHarmonah) is obscure — possibly a place name, a word for the dung heap, or a geographical term for the Hermon range north of Israel. The image of women being pushed through the city's breached walls during conquest is consistent with ANE military descriptions of siege and conquest. Samaria's walls were eventually breached by Sargon II's Assyrian army in 722/720 BCE after a three-year siege (2 Kings 17:5-6).
4: 'Come to Bethel and rebel; go to Gilgal and multiply your rebellion; bring your sacrifices every morning, your tithes every three years.' The bitter sarcasm of the invitation to continue the cult at Israel's two major sanctuaries (Bethel and Gilgal were both associated with Jacob-traditions and Israelite history — Genesis 12:8; Joshua 4:19; 1 Samuel 11:15) is characteristic of Amos's rhetorical style. 'Rebel' (pish'u) is the same word used throughout the nation-oracle formula ('for three crimes'); the cult itself, Amos says, is the site of rebellion. The specific acts mentioned — daily sacrifices, triennial tithes — were the required cultic obligations, but performed in the context of social injustice they become rebellion.
5: 'Offer your leavened thanksgiving sacrifices and proclaim your freewill offerings, announce them publicly — for so you love to do, O people of Israel, declares the Lord GOD.' The command to publicly announce freewill offerings is sarcasm targeting the public display of piety: those who 'love' to announce their generosity are performing religious theater rather than worshipping YHWH. Leavened bread was normally prohibited in sacrifices (Leviticus 2:11) except in certain thanksgiving contexts (Leviticus 7:13); the specific mention may be pointing to liturgical irregularities at Bethel. The phrase 'for so you love to do' drips with contempt for performative religion.
6: 'I gave you empty mouths in all your cities and shortage of food in all your towns — yet you did not return to me (velo shavtem adai), declares the LORD.' The first of five covenant-discipline episodes (vv6-11), each ending with the refrain 'yet you did not return to me,' forms a theological argument: YHWH used calibrated hardship (famine, drought, blight, plague, partial destruction) as covenant correctives before bringing total judgment. The 'empty mouths' (niqayon shinayim, literally 'cleanness/emptiness of teeth') is a vivid idiom for famine: teeth with nothing to chew on. The refrain connects to Hosea's theology (Hosea 6:1: 'come, let us return to the LORD').
7: 'I also withheld the rain from you three months before harvest; I sent rain on one city but not on another city; one field would receive rain while the field that received no rain would dry up.' The selective pattern of rain and drought — one city receiving rain while another goes dry, one field watered while its neighbor bakes — is a remarkable description of the micro-climatic agricultural conditions of the Levant, where hills and valleys can have dramatically different rainfall. This was understood as YHWH's control over the weather (cf. Deuteronomy 28:23-24: 'the sky over your head will be bronze, the ground beneath your feet iron'). Archaeological grain-storage studies confirm periodic drought crises in Iron Age II Palestine.
8: 'Two or three cities staggered to another city for water and could not get enough — yet you did not return to me.' The image of urban populations physically migrating to seek water is consistent with the water-dependence of ancient Levantine cities on springs, cisterns, and seasonal rainfall. Samaria's water supply was partly dependent on the Wadi Farah and other seasonal sources; in drought years, the economic and human cost would be severe. The 'staggering' (nau, wandered/staggered) reflects physical exhaustion alongside the drought's economic impact.
9: 'I struck your crops with blight and mildew; locusts devoured your many gardens and vineyards, your fig trees and olive trees.' Agricultural blight (shidafon, scorching wind) and mildew (yraqon, yellowing/mildew) are paired in the covenant-curse formulas of Deuteronomy 28:22 and in Solomon's temple prayer (1 Kings 8:37). Locust plagues were catastrophic and well-documented throughout ancient Near Eastern records; the Joel 1-2 locust description provides the most detailed biblical account. The specific mention of gardens, vineyards, fig trees, and olive trees — the four main crops of ancient Israelite agriculture — shows that the entire agricultural system was struck.
10: 'I sent among you a plague like those of Egypt (bedeqer Mitzraim); I killed your young men with the sword, took your horses away, and made the stench of your camp go up into your nostrils.' The 'plague like those of Egypt' deliberately invokes the Exodus narrative in reverse: the same plagues YHWH used to liberate Israel from Egypt are now turned against Israel in its own land. The sword deaths and capture of horses suggest specific military encounters — probably border raids by Arameans or other opponents during periods of weakness. The 'stench of the camp' after a military defeat is the putrefaction of unburied bodies.
11: 'I overthrew some of you as God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah; you were like a stick snatched from the fire.' The Sodom-and-Gomorrah comparison is the most extreme point on the covenant-curse spectrum — the paradigmatic destruction that YHWH uses as the standard for ultimate judgment (Genesis 19:24-25; Deuteronomy 29:23; Isaiah 1:9; Jeremiah 49:18). The 'stick snatched from the fire' (ud mutzal misreifah) is a remnant-after-disaster image: some survived but only as barely-saved debris. This phrase recurs in Zechariah 3:2 for Joshua the high priest's restoration after exile. Yet even this extreme near-destruction 'did not cause you to return' — the refrain's final occurrence before the threat shifts to total judgment.
12: 'Therefore, because I will do this to you, O Israel, prepare yourself to meet your God.' The 'this' is deliberately vague — after the catalogue of graduated covenant disciplines, the next judgment is something beyond the list, implied to be worse than Sodom's destruction. 'Prepare to meet your God' (hikkon liqrat Eloheicha Yisrael) is the most terrifying summons in Amos — framed like a theophanic preparation (Exodus 19:11: 'be ready for the third day, for on the third day the LORD will come down'), but here directed toward judgment rather than covenant. The verse refuses to name the judgment, forcing the listener's imagination to supply it.
13: 'For this is he who forms the mountains and creates the wind, who reveals to humanity what he is thinking, who turns dawn into darkness and treads on the heights of the earth — the LORD God Almighty is his name.' The doxology closes Amos 4 with a hymn to YHWH's creative sovereignty. Three creation acts (forming mountains, creating wind, turning dawn to darkness) and one relational act (revealing his thoughts to humanity through prophecy) establish that the God who is about to judge Israel is the Creator. The triple divine title at the close — 'the LORD God Almighty' (YHWH Elohei Tzvaot shemo) — is the fullest possible expression of divine authority in Amos's vocabulary.
